subject: EU Law
lesson_title: The Customs Union and Quantitative Restrictions: Dassonville and the Prohibition Machinery

questions.question: Articles 28–30 TFEU establish the customs union. What does the customs union require?

options.A: That member states adopt identical tariff rates for all goods traded between them
options.B: The prohibition of customs duties and charges having equivalent effect (CHEEs) between member states, and the adoption of a common customs tariff in relations with third countries
options.C: That member states remove all non-tariff barriers to trade but may maintain national tariff schedules
options.D: That all goods manufactured in the EU move freely, but goods imported from third countries may be restricted by individual member states

questions.explanation: The customs union has two components: (1) the internal dimension — prohibition of customs duties and CHEEs between member states (Articles 28–30 TFEU); and (2) the external dimension — a common customs tariff (CCT) applied uniformly by all member states to goods imported from third countries (Article 31 TFEU). Once goods from third countries are lawfully in free circulation in one member state (customs cleared, CCT paid), they can move freely throughout the EU. The customs union is more than a free trade area because it requires uniformity in relation to third countries.

questions.question: A 'charge having equivalent effect' (CHEE) to a customs duty is prohibited under Article 30 TFEU. The CJEU defined CHEEs in which case?

options.A: Commission v Italy (Statistical Levy) [1969] — any pecuniary charge, however small, imposed unilaterally on goods crossing a border by reason of that fact alone, constitutes a CHEE
options.B: Cassis de Dijon [1979] — any charge on imported goods that distorts competition
options.C: Dassonville [1974] — any measure capable of hindering intra-EU trade
options.D: Van Gend en Loos [1963] — any charge imposed on goods in breach of direct effect

questions.explanation: Commission v Italy (Statistical Levy) [1969] (Case 24/68) established the broad definition of CHEE: any pecuniary charge, however small and whatever its designation and mode of application, which is imposed unilaterally on domestic or foreign goods by reason of the fact that they cross a frontier, and which is not a customs duty in the strict sense. Only three narrow exceptions escape classification as a CHEE: charges for a service genuinely rendered to the individual importer at the border (at cost); charges required by EU law; and charges forming part of a general internal taxation system applied systematically (but then assessed under Article 110 TFEU, not Article 30).

questions.question: Article 34 TFEU prohibits 'quantitative restrictions and all measures having equivalent effect' (MEEs) on imports. The foundational definition of an MEE was given in:

options.A: Keck and Mithouard [1993] — a measure that directly prevents imports
options.B: Dassonville [1974] — 'all trading rules enacted by member states which are capable of hindering, directly or indirectly, actually or potentially, intra-Community trade'
options.C: Commission v Ireland (Irish Souvenirs) [1981] — rules requiring goods to carry marks of origin
options.D: Cassis de Dijon [1979] — rules imposing mandatory requirements on product composition

questions.explanation: Dassonville (Case 8/74, 1974) is the foundational case on MEEs. The CJEU held that all trading rules enacted by member states which are capable of hindering, directly or indirectly, actually or potentially, intra-Community trade are to be considered as MEEs. This is an extraordinarily wide formula — it captures measures that affect trade only potentially or indirectly. The breadth of the Dassonville formula was later partly curtailed by Keck (for selling arrangements) and by the mandatory requirements doctrine in Cassis. The formula applies to actual and potential, direct and indirect, restrictions.

questions.question: The Dassonville formula is notably broad. What is the practical consequence of this breadth?

options.A: Almost any national measure affecting trade in goods is caught — requiring member states to justify it under Article 36 or the mandatory requirements doctrine
options.B: Only measures specifically targeting imported goods are caught by Article 34
options.C: The formula is limited to discriminatory measures — non-discriminatory measures are excluded
options.D: Member states have full discretion to regulate the internal market provided they notify the Commission

questions.explanation: The breadth of Dassonville means that virtually any national rule with any effect on trade could be caught by Article 34. This imposes a heavy burden on member states to justify their regulatory choices under either Article 36 TFEU (express derogations) or the Cassis mandatory requirements doctrine (imperative requirements). The width of Dassonville was partly responsible for the CJEU's subsequent carve-out in Keck — as the Court recognised that the formula was being used to challenge commercial regulations that had no real protectionist effect, creating an unacceptable burden on national regulatory autonomy.

questions.question: A 'quantitative restriction' on imports under Article 34 TFEU means:

options.A: Only total bans on the import of a good
options.B: Any measure that amounts to a total or partial restraint on imports — including quotas, import bans, and licensing systems that limit the quantity of goods that may be imported
options.C: Only measures imposing numerical limits but not total bans
options.D: Only measures imposed at the border

questions.explanation: A quantitative restriction (QR) is any measure that amounts to a total or partial restraint on the import, export, or transit of goods. This covers: total import bans; import quotas (numerical limits); and licensing systems that have the effect of limiting imports to a fixed quantity. In Geddo v Ente Nazionale Risi [1973], the CJEU confirmed that measures constituting a complete or partial restraint on imports constitute a QR. Article 34 covers QRs and MEEs on imports; Article 35 covers exports; Article 36 provides express derogations for both.

questions.question: Article 35 TFEU prohibits quantitative restrictions and MEEs on exports. The CJEU in Groenveld [1979] (C-15/79) held that Article 35 applies:

options.A: To any national measure that restricts exports in any way
options.B: Only to national measures that specifically aim to restrict exports or discriminate against exports — not to genuinely non-discriminatory measures that affect domestic and exported goods identically
options.C: Only to measures targeting goods exported to specific member states
options.D: To the same range of measures as Article 34 — both have identical scope

questions.explanation: Groenveld (and the subsequent refinement in Gysbrechts [2008]) established that Article 35 has a narrower scope than Article 34: it applies only to national measures that have as their specific object or effect the restriction of patterns of exports, creating a difference in treatment between the domestic trade and the export trade. Purely indistinctly applicable measures that affect domestic and export goods identically are generally outside Article 35. This contrasts with Article 34 which (post-Dassonville) captures even indistinctly applicable measures that merely have a potential restricting effect on imports.

questions.question: In Commission v Ireland (Buying Irish / Irish Souvenirs) [1981] (Case 249/81), the CJEU held that a government-backed 'Buy Irish' campaign amounted to an MEE. Why?

options.A: Because the campaign imposed a tax on imported goods
options.B: Because even non-binding measures — government campaigns, promotional materials, and advertising by public bodies — can amount to MEEs if they are capable of influencing consumer behaviour and thereby restricting imports
options.C: Because the campaign used EU funds to promote national goods
options.D: Because Irish consumers were legally required to purchase Irish goods under the scheme

questions.explanation: Commission v Ireland (Buying Irish) confirmed that Article 34 is not limited to legally binding measures — it captures any state measure (even non-binding promotional campaigns, government advertising, and public body initiatives) that is capable of hindering imports. The Irish government's 'Buy Irish' campaign, though not legally binding, was state-sponsored, systematic, and clearly capable of diverting consumer preferences toward domestic products. The CJEU held that the measure constituted an MEE regardless of its non-mandatory character. State responsibility extends to bodies acting under government authority.

questions.question: The 'origin marking' requirement — requiring goods to display their country of origin — has been assessed under Article 34. The general rule from Commission v UK [1985] (Origin Marking) is:

options.A: Mandatory origin marking requirements are lawful as they inform consumers
options.B: Mandatory origin marking requirements are MEEs — they enable consumers to discriminate against foreign goods and may stigmatise imports; they are not justified under Article 36 or mandatory requirements
options.C: Origin marking requirements are lawful if applied to both domestic and imported goods
options.D: Origin marking requirements are outside Article 34 because they are information requirements, not trade restrictions

questions.explanation: Commission v UK (Origin Marking) established that requiring goods to carry 'Made in UK' or 'Made in [Country X]' markings is an MEE. Such requirements enable and may encourage consumers to discriminate against imported goods — they facilitate market partitioning along national lines. The CJEU held that origin marking does not serve a legitimate consumer protection interest (which would be served by identifying the manufacturer, not the country of origin). This illustrates that even indistinctly applicable measures can be MEEs if they have the practical effect of favouring domestic goods.

questions.question: Article 36 TFEU permits member states to derogate from Articles 34–35 TFEU on certain grounds. Which of the following is NOT an express derogation ground under Article 36?

options.B: Protection of industrial and commercial property

questions.explanation: Article 36 TFEU provides an exhaustive list of permitted derogation grounds: public morality; public policy; public security; protection of health and life of humans, animals, or plants; protection of national treasures of artistic, historic or archaeological value; protection of industrial and commercial property. Consumer protection is NOT an express Article 36 ground — it is, however, a recognised mandatory requirement under the Cassis de Dijon doctrine (an unwritten judge-made derogation for indistinctly applicable measures). The exhaustive nature of Article 36 grounds means member states cannot add new grounds to it — only the CJEU has developed the supplementary Cassis doctrine.

questions.question: Even where a member state successfully invokes an Article 36 TFEU derogation ground, the measure must satisfy two further requirements. What are they?

options.A: The measure must be proportionate and must not constitute arbitrary discrimination or a disguised restriction on trade between member states
options.B: The measure must be notified to the Commission and approved before taking effect
options.C: The measure must be applied only to goods from non-EU countries, not to goods from other member states
options.D: The measure must have been in force before the EU Treaty was ratified by the member state

questions.explanation: Article 36 contains its own safeguard in its second sentence: 'Such prohibitions or restrictions shall not, however, constitute a means of arbitrary discrimination or a disguised restriction on trade between member states.' Even if a derogation ground is established, the measure: (1) must not be applied in an arbitrarily discriminatory manner (e.g., applied to foreign goods but not domestic goods raising the same concern); (2) must not be a disguised restriction on trade (a pretext for protectionism); and (3) must be proportionate to the aim pursued — the CJEU applies a proportionality analysis examining whether the measure is suitable and necessary to achieve the legitimate aim.

questions.question: The principle of proportionality as applied to Article 36 TFEU derogations requires the member state to show:

options.A: That the measure is the cheapest option available to achieve the regulatory objective
options.B: That the measure is appropriate (suitable) to achieve the legitimate aim and does not go beyond what is necessary — member states must consider whether less trade-restrictive alternatives could achieve the same protection
options.C: That the measure was adopted in consultation with affected traders
options.D: That the measure applies equally to all goods regardless of origin

questions.explanation: Proportionality in the Article 36 context requires: (1) suitability — the measure must be capable of achieving the stated derogation objective; (2) necessity — the measure must not go beyond what is necessary; less trade-restrictive means of achieving the same level of protection must be absent or inadequate. The CJEU does not require that the measure be the 'least restrictive' possible in absolute terms, but it scrutinises whether alternative measures could achieve equivalent protection with less trade restriction. The burden of justification lies with the member state. Excessive or disproportionate measures will fail the Article 36 test even if the ground in principle is available.

questions.question: The 'public health' derogation under Article 36 TFEU has been widely invoked. Which principle governs member state reliance on public health justifications for restricting imports?

options.A: Member states have absolute discretion on public health matters — the CJEU cannot review the scientific basis of health measures
options.B: Member states may determine the level of protection they wish to afford public health, but the scientific basis of the measure must be credible and the measure proportionate — precautionary measures are permitted but must rest on a genuine risk assessment
options.C: Public health justifications are only available where the WHO has declared a health emergency
options.D: Public health justifications apply only to restrictions on food imports, not other goods

questions.explanation: Member states retain significant autonomy in determining the level of health protection they wish to achieve. However, the CJEU requires that health-based restrictions be founded on reliable scientific evidence of a genuine health risk. Purely speculative or unsubstantiated health fears will not suffice. The precautionary principle permits member states to act before full scientific certainty is established where there is a plausible, credible risk. The measure must still be proportionate. Health claims cannot be used as a pretext for protectionism — the CJEU scrutinises whether the measure genuinely serves health protection or simply discriminates against imports.

questions.question: A member state imposes a licensing requirement on all imports of a product, applying the same rules to domestic producers. Under Article 34 TFEU, this measure:

options.A: Is automatically lawful as it applies to both domestic and imported goods
options.B: May still constitute an MEE — an indistinctly applicable measure (applying equally to imports and domestic goods) can be an MEE if it is capable of hindering intra-EU trade; it must then be justified under Article 36 or the Cassis mandatory requirements doctrine
options.C: Is outside Article 34 because it does not discriminate
options.D: Is automatically unlawful as any licensing system constitutes a quantitative restriction

questions.explanation: Post-Dassonville, Article 34 captures indistinctly applicable measures — rules that apply equally to domestic and imported goods. Such measures can hinder trade because imported goods may already comply with their home state's rules and face double regulatory burdens when entering the import state. Cassis de Dijon then provides the framework for justifying indistinctly applicable MEEs via mandatory requirements. The critical distinction is: distinctly applicable measures (applying only to imports) can only be justified under Article 36; indistinctly applicable measures can additionally be justified under the Cassis mandatory requirements doctrine — subject to proportionality.
